Using DOMPurify in Vue 3 to Prevent XSS Attacks
lets you render an HTML string as actual DOM. The catch is that this opens up XSS attacks — you can't be sure user input is safe, so you need to filter input before rendering it.

Quick note: per the official docs,

In real-world use you usually don't want to filter everything — some tags should be allowed. Customise with the second argument to
This part is pretty involved — just refer to the
The common case is user-supplied input — Editors, comment boxes, etc. Modern frameworks have basic XSS defence built in, and the backend usually sanitises data before storage too.
So if both the backend and the framework already filter, do you still need
? Depends on the use case. In Vue, if you render with
, you don't need DOMPurify — Vue handles it. But if you use
, you do — DOMPurify filters out dangerous tags so malicious scripts can't execute.
In short: if your feature renders user-supplied HTML directly, sanitise it with DOMPurify on the frontend to keep malicious scripts from executing.

In Vue, `v-html` turns an HTML string into actual DOM. That introduces XSS risk — there's no guarantee user input is safe, so we need to sanitise it to prevent malicious scripts from executing.
